content.text: A multi-quarter roadmap for a Claude enterprise program communicates the program's direction over four to six quarters with decreasing specificity as the horizon grows. The near-term quarters are committed and detailed; the far quarters are directional and thematic. This graduated confidence model allows executives to see the program's trajectory while avoiding the waste of detailed planning for work that will change before it is executed.

attrs.alt: Four-quarter Claude program roadmap organized by themes (Adoption, Infrastructure, FinOps, Safety) with graduated planning confidence

content.text: The most common roadmap failure is treating all four quarters as equally certain. The quarterly planning effort that goes into Q3 should not be the same as the effort for Q2 of next year. The graduated confidence model makes this explicit.
These ship. If they don't, there will be a retrospective.
High probability. Will be committed at quarter-start planning.
Medium probability. Direction is set; initiatives are not.
Low probability. Communicates vision, not commitment.

When stakeholders ask 'why don't you know exactly what you're building in Q2 2027?', the answer is: because we don't know what we'll learn in Q3 2026. A roadmap that pretends certainty for 18 months is not a plan — it is a fiction that will be completely revised within two quarters. The graduated model is honest about what is and is not known.

# roadmap_model.py
# Data model for a multi-quarter Claude program roadmap.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Confidence(str, Enum):
    COMMITTED    = "committed"    # Q-current: fully planned, engineer assigned
    PLANNED      = "planned"      # Q+1: prioritized, likely to be worked
    DIRECTIONAL  = "directional"  # Q+2: theme confirmed, scope not final
    ASPIRATIONAL = "aspirational" # Q+3+: vision only, no capacity committed

class Theme(str, Enum):
    ADOPTION     = "adoption"
    INFRA        = "infrastructure"
    FINOPS       = "finops"
    SAFETY       = "safety"
    PLATFORM     = "platform"

@dataclass
class RoadmapItem:
    item_id: str
    title: str
    description: str
    quarter: str             # "Q3-2026"
    theme: Theme
    confidence: Confidence
    expected_outcome: str    # what KR does this move?
    owner: str
    initiative_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # links to portfolio

@dataclass
class QuarterTheme:
    quarter: str
    theme_name: str          # e.g. "Foundation", "Scale"
    theme_description: str   # one sentence describing the quarter's focus
    north_star: str          # single most important outcome for the quarter

@dataclass
class ProgramRoadmap:
    program_name: str
    version: str             # "v2.1" — increment on each refresh
    last_updated: str        # ISO date
    quarters: list[QuarterTheme]
    items: list[RoadmapItem]

    def items_by_quarter(self, quarter: str) -> list[RoadmapItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.quarter == quarter]

    def items_by_theme(self, theme: Theme) -> list[RoadmapItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.theme == theme]

    def committed_items(self) -> list[RoadmapItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.confidence == Confidence.COMMITTED]

Each RoadmapItem links back to the portfolio through initiative_ids. This means the roadmap is not a separate document — it is a view over the portfolio. When an initiative is marked complete in the portfolio tracker, the roadmap item it belongs to automatically reflects that. Keeping these in sync prevents the common problem of a roadmap that shows items as 'planned' that the portfolio shows as already done or cancelled.

Each quarter has a one or two word theme that communicates the primary focus. The theme is not a constraint on what the team builds — it is a communication device that helps stakeholders understand the program's evolution at a glance. A sequence like 'Foundation → Scale → Optimise → Intelligence' tells a coherent story even to someone who hasn't read the underlying initiatives.
First 1–2 quarters of a new program
Gateway, logging, policy, intake portal, first 5–10 adoptions
Onboarding automation, shared libraries, 10–25 team adoptions
After scale, when cost is the priority
Caching playbooks, batch routing, model selection, FinOps KPIs
Mature program with stable cost base
ROI measurement, advanced evals, fine-tuning, platform APIs
Triggered by regulatory or audit requirement
SOC2, data residency, audit logging, red-team program

# theme_generator.py
# Use Claude to suggest roadmap themes based on current portfolio and OKRs.
# Input: current quarter's initiatives + OKR progress. Output: theme name + focus areas.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def suggest_quarter_theme(
    quarter: str,
    completed_items: list[dict],     # items from the prior quarter
    pending_high_priority: list[dict],  # P0/P1 items not yet done
    program_okrs: list[dict],         # current program OKRs with progress
) -> dict:
    """
    Suggest a quarterly theme (name + 3 focus areas) based on what's been delivered
    and what the OKRs need next.
    """
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        system=(
            "You are an AI program strategist. Suggest a quarterly theme for a Claude "
            "enterprise program. Output JSON: "
            "{"theme_name": "one or two words", "
            ""theme_description": "one sentence", "
            ""focus_areas": ["string", "string", "string"], "
            ""north_star": "the single most important outcome"}"
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Quarter: {quarter}\n"
                f"Completed last quarter: {json.dumps(completed_items)}\n"
                f"High-priority backlog: {json.dumps(pending_high_priority)}\n"
                f"OKR progress: {json.dumps(program_okrs)}"
            ),
        }],
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

The roadmap is a living document that is reviewed monthly, not just at quarter boundaries. A monthly health check flags confidence mismatches — items labelled 'planned' for next quarter that have no portfolio link and no owner assigned — and checks whether committed items are on track to deliver their expected outcomes.

# roadmap_review.py
# Monthly roadmap health check: are delivered items matching the quarter theme?
# Are confidence levels still accurate for future quarters?

from dataclasses import dataclass
from roadmap_model import ProgramRoadmap, Confidence, RoadmapItem

@dataclass
class RoadmapHealthReport:
    quarter: str
    committed_total: int
    committed_done: int
    committed_on_track: int
    committed_at_risk: int
    directional_count: int   # Q+2 items
    confidence_downgrades: list[str]  # items that should be downgraded

def assess_roadmap_health(
    roadmap: ProgramRoadmap,
    current_quarter: str,
    completed_item_ids: set[str],
    at_risk_item_ids: set[str],
) -> RoadmapHealthReport:
    committed = [i for i in roadmap.committed_items() if i.quarter == current_quarter]
    done = [i for i in committed if i.item_id in completed_item_ids]
    at_risk = [i for i in committed if i.item_id in at_risk_item_ids]

    # Flag planned items that should be downgraded to directional
    # if we are past week 9 and they haven't been assigned yet
    directional = roadmap.items_by_quarter(
        _next_quarter(current_quarter)
    )
    should_downgrade = [
        i.title for i in directional
        if i.confidence == Confidence.PLANNED
        and not i.initiative_ids  # no portfolio link = not yet sized
    ]

    return RoadmapHealthReport(
        quarter=current_quarter,
        committed_total=len(committed),
        committed_done=len(done),
        committed_on_track=len(committed) - len(at_risk) - len(done),
        committed_at_risk=len(at_risk),
        directional_count=len(directional),
        confidence_downgrades=should_downgrade,
    )

def _next_quarter(q: str) -> str:
    """e.g. "Q3-2026" -> "Q4-2026", "Q4-2026" -> "Q1-2027" """
    n, y = q.split("-")
    n_num = int(n[1])
    if n_num < 4:
        return f"Q{n_num+1}-{y}"
    return f"Q1-{int(y)+1}"

Any item labelled 'planned' for Q+1 that reaches week 10 of the current quarter without an assigned engineer and portfolio link must be downgraded to 'directional.' This prevents the next quarter's committed list from starting overloaded.
If more than 40% of committed items for a quarter do not match the quarter's stated theme, the theme is wrong — either update the theme name or reschedule the off-theme items.
Every refresh of the roadmap increments the version number. When executives compare two roadmap documents and ask 'why did X move?', the version number tells them which document is current.

The full roadmap with initiative IDs, effort estimates, and internal owners is for the program team. Stakeholders — executives, finance, external partners — get a narrative summary that focuses on outcomes and direction, not implementation detail.

# stakeholder_roadmap.py
# Generate a stakeholder-facing roadmap summary (no internal detail).
# Strips initiative IDs, effort estimates, and internal owners.

import anthropic
import json
from roadmap_model import ProgramRoadmap, Confidence

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def generate_stakeholder_summary(
    roadmap: ProgramRoadmap,
    audience: str,   # "VP Engineering" | "Finance" | "All-hands"
) -> str:
    """
    Generate a 3-paragraph stakeholder narrative from the roadmap data.
    Paragraph 1: what ships this quarter (committed).
    Paragraph 2: what's on deck next quarter (planned).
    Paragraph 3: the 1-year direction (directional + aspirational).
    """
    committed = [
        {"title": i.title, "outcome": i.expected_outcome}
        for i in roadmap.items
        if i.confidence == Confidence.COMMITTED
    ]
    planned = [
        {"title": i.title, "quarter": i.quarter}
        for i in roadmap.items
        if i.confidence == Confidence.PLANNED
    ]
    future = [
        {"title": i.title, "quarter": i.quarter}
        for i in roadmap.items
        if i.confidence in (Confidence.DIRECTIONAL, Confidence.ASPIRATIONAL)
    ]

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=500,
        system=(
            f"You are writing a roadmap summary for {audience}. "
            "Write 3 paragraphs: (1) what we're delivering now, (2) what's next, "
            "(3) the year direction. Be concrete about outcomes, not features. "
            "Avoid jargon. No bullet points."
        ),
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Committed this quarter: {json.dumps(committed)}\n"
                f"Planned next quarter: {json.dumps(planned)}\n"
                f"Future direction: {json.dumps(future)}"
            ),
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
All 4 quarters; committed detail + planned summary
Current quarter theme + one north-star outcome for next quarter
Aspirational only — no internal initiative detail
'Build batch API integration' is a feature. 'Reduce support team's monthly AI cost by $15K' is an outcome. Stakeholders care about outcomes.
The most common mistake. Planning the next quarter's committed list before the current quarter's results are in leads to scope creep at the next planning cycle.
A roadmap is a current best guess, not a promise. Make this explicit at every distribution. Items move; confidence is what's promised, not the specific items.
When the theme shifts from 'Scale' to 'Optimise', explain the trigger. Budget pressure? Competitive pressure? Regulatory requirement? The narrative around a theme change builds trust.
Every roadmap item should name the OKR KR it moves. A roadmap item that doesn't connect to any KR is scope that the program cannot justify in a budget review.
Roadmap as a YAML File in Version Control

Storing the roadmap as a YAML file in the same repository as the program's code and OKR definitions makes it searchable, diffable, and auditable. When an executive asks 'when did the Q2 2027 AI ROI dashboard first appear on the roadmap?', the git log answers in seconds. A slide deck cannot do this.

# program_roadmap_Q3_2026.yaml
# Source-of-truth roadmap file — checked into version control alongside code.
# Increment "version" on every monthly refresh.

program: "Claude Enterprise Program"
version: "v1.0"
last_updated: "2026-07-01"
owner: "AI Program Lead"

quarters:
  - id: "Q3-2026"
    theme_name: "Foundation"
    theme_description: "Stand up the core infrastructure and governance that all future quarters build on."
    north_star: "15 teams actively using Claude in production by Sep 30"
    confidence: committed

  - id: "Q4-2026"
    theme_name: "Scale"
    theme_description: "Expand adoption from early adopters to the broad engineering organisation."
    north_star: "25 teams on Claude; self-serve onboarding live"
    confidence: planned

  - id: "Q1-2027"
    theme_name: "Optimise"
    theme_description: "Drive cost efficiency and quality improvements across all live use cases."
    north_star: "Cost per conversation ≤ $0.020 across all teams"
    confidence: directional

  - id: "Q2-2027"
    theme_name: "Intelligence"
    theme_description: "Build higher-order capabilities: ROI measurement, advanced routing, platform APIs."
    north_star: "AI ROI dashboard live; revenue-per-dollar tracked for top 10 use cases"
    confidence: aspirational

items:
  # Q3 2026 — COMMITTED
  - id: "RI-001"
    title: "Shared API gateway with usage logging"
    quarter: "Q3-2026"
    theme: infrastructure
    confidence: committed
    expected_outcome: "Enables usage attribution by team (required for FinOps KRs)"
    owner: "Platform CoE"
    okr_kr: "Cache hit rate ≥ 70% org-wide"

  - id: "RI-002"
    title: "Executive FinOps dashboard v1"
    quarter: "Q3-2026"
    theme: finops
    confidence: committed
    expected_outcome: "Budget owners have real-time spend visibility"
    owner: "FinOps Lead"
    okr_kr: "Cost per conversation ≤ $0.025"

  - id: "RI-003"
    title: "Usage policy and PII guardrails"
    quarter: "Q3-2026"
    theme: safety
    confidence: committed
    expected_outcome: "Compliance risk reduced; enables regulated-data use cases"
    owner: "Security + CoE"
    okr_kr: "All teams pass security review before go-live"

  # Q4 2026 — PLANNED
  - id: "RI-010"
    title: "Self-serve team onboarding portal"
    quarter: "Q4-2026"
    theme: adoption
    confidence: planned
    expected_outcome: "Removes CoE bottleneck; teams can start independently"
    owner: "CoE"
    okr_kr: "25 teams on Claude by Dec 31"

  - id: "RI-011"
    title: "Shared prompt library v1"
    quarter: "Q4-2026"
    theme: infrastructure
    confidence: planned
    expected_outcome: "Reduces duplicate prompt engineering; accelerates onboarding"
    owner: "CoE"
    okr_kr: "Avg time to production ≤ 4 weeks"

The YAML file is the source of truth. Slide decks, Confluence pages, and stakeholder emails are generated from it — never the other way around. When the roadmap changes, update the YAML first, then regenerate the derived artifacts. This eliminates the common problem of three different roadmap versions circulating simultaneously.
Increment on every monthly refresh. Stakeholders reference the version number when asking questions about specific roadmap states.
Links the roadmap item to the specific KR it moves. An item with no okr_kr link has no programmatic justification and should be challenged at the next review.

The quarter-level confidence is the overall confidence for that quarter. Individual items can have lower confidence than their quarter (a 'planned' quarter can have one 'directional' item that hasn't been sized yet).
Item status updated; at-risk flags raised
Version incremented; confidence levels reviewed; Q+1 items sized
Q+1 moved to committed; Q+2 moved to planned; Q+3 directional themes set
When a major P0 arrives or capacity changes
Current quarter re-committed; downstream quarters adjusted

The monthly refresh is the highest-value habit in roadmap management. Thirty minutes each month to review confidence levels, check for orphaned items (no OKR link, no owner), and update the version prevents the gradual drift that makes roadmaps untrustworthy. A roadmap that was last updated four months ago is a historical document, not a planning tool.

A multi-quarter Claude program roadmap uses graduated confidence — committed for the current quarter, planned for Q+1, directional for Q+2, and aspirational beyond — to communicate the program's direction honestly. Each quarter has a theme that tells a coherent story across four quarters even to stakeholders who haven't read the underlying initiatives. The roadmap is versioned, linked to the portfolio, and reviewed monthly for confidence mismatches and theme drift. Stakeholder-facing summaries are generated as outcome narratives, not feature lists. Every item connects to a program OKR KR so the roadmap can be defended in a budget review.
